cues.label: Which evals to build
cues.headline: Good plumbing cannot save a wrong test
cues.narration: Even with perfect reproducible plumbing, the team found their existing evals were quietly missing the failures that mattered most.

cues.label: Single-turn versus multi-turn
cues.headline: One shape is blind to the other
cues.narration: Single-turn grades one answer in isolation, while a multi-turn agentic task carries state across turns and a tool call can span turns.

cues.label: The benchmarks that expose it
cues.headline: Grade the world, match the use
cues.narration: The Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard version three adds multi-turn and state checks, while FunctionGemma docs promise only single and parallel calls.

cues.label: The template trap
cues.headline: Ten percent was a formatting bug
cues.narration: At the Gemma 4 launch tool calls passed around ten percent, but the weights were fine and a hand-tuned chat template lifted the rate dramatically.

cues.label: What caught the nuance
cues.headline: The right eval turned guess into proof
cues.narration: Academic single-turn evals only reported a low number, while Frank's agentic benchmark showed the failures were formatting and justified the rollout.

cues.label: Why the teams disagreed
cues.headline: What counts as a real eval is a decision
cues.narration: The post-training team said multi-turn was out of scope while the evals team saw users depend on it, so the blind spot was a position somebody held.

cues.label: Keeping the score honest
cues.headline: Seeded state and held-out discipline
cues.narration: A seeded environment grades on final state, and holding eval data out of training defeats contamination that would inflate the number into a lie.

cues.label: An eval worth building
cues.headline: Disclosure by audience
cues.narration: A context-disclosure privacy eval would score whether an agent reveals the right amount to a wife, a friend, a colleague, or a stranger, worth exploring later.
